record_office_terminology.land_records: Clerk of Circuit Court (deeds, mortgages, liens); Maryland State Archives has digitized land records for all counties on mdlandrec.net
record_office_terminology.probate: Register of Wills (wills, estates); Orphans' Court (guardianships, minors' estates, apprenticeships); both at county level
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Clerk of Circuit Court (marriage licenses from colonial era); county-level filing
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1898 (state registration of births and deaths began; earlier records primarily in church records and some county filings)

burned_county_authority: Maryland State Archives (MSA), Annapolis — MSA is the central repository for all Maryland county and state records. Maryland has only one recognized burned county (Calvert, 1882 courthouse fire). MSA holds state-level copies and colonial records that help reconstruct losses. Maryland's record survival is generally excellent.
administrative_notes: Maryland was founded in 1634 as a proprietary colony by Cecilius Calvert, 2nd Baron Baltimore, as a haven for English Catholics. It has 23 counties plus Baltimore City (independent since 1851) for 24 total jurisdictions. Maryland was one of the 13 original colonies. It was a slave state that did NOT secede during the Civil War — it remained a border state, though sympathies were deeply divided. The Maryland State Archives (MSA) in Annapolis is the central state repository and one of the best-organized state archives in the US. MSA's mdlandrec.net provides free online access to digitized land records for ALL Maryland counties — a national model. The Clerk of Circuit Court in each county handles land records, while the Register of Wills handles probate and the Orphans' Court handles estates and guardianships. Vital records registration began in 1898; before that, church records are the primary source for births, marriages, and deaths. Maryland's Catholic heritage (Archdiocese of Baltimore, Jesuit missions) provides uniquely rich Catholic sacramental records. Baltimore was one of the nation's top immigration ports, second only to New York/Ellis Island. The Eastern Shore vs Western Shore distinction is important genealogically — they developed as distinct cultural regions.

core_repositories.description: The central repository for Maryland's historical and governmental records. Holds county records (deeds, probate, court), colonial records from the 1630s, vital records, military records, church record copies, and the 'Legacy of Slavery in Maryland' database. MSA operates mdlandrec.net for free online land record access. Research room in Annapolis is open to the public. One of the best-organized state archives in the US.

core_repositories.source_name: Maryland Center for History and Culture (formerly Maryland Historical Society)
core_repositories.repository: Maryland Center for History and Culture, Baltimore
core_repositories.description: Major manuscript and library repository in Baltimore. Holds family papers, business records, church records, photographs, maps, and published genealogies. Extensive collections on Baltimore history, Civil War in Maryland, and Maryland families. The H. Furlong Baldwin Library has genealogical materials and Maryland periodicals.

core_repositories.source_name: Enoch Pratt Free Library — Maryland Department
core_repositories.description: Baltimore's public library system has an excellent Maryland Department with city directories from the 1790s, newspapers, maps, photographs, and Maryland genealogical publications. Essential for Baltimore City research. Free public access.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Maryland county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at MSA and county offices. Key collections include Maryland marriages, probate records, and church records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized records. Covers Maryland's unique record types, repositories, and research strategies including the MSA archives system.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Maryland newspapers from the 1700s. Includes Baltimore Sun, Frederick newspapers, Eastern Shore weeklies, and county papers. Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, runaway slave ads, and community news.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Maryland Digital Newspaper Project — University of Maryland
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: University of Maryland's digitized newspaper collections including papers from throughout the state. Part of the National Digital Newspaper Program. Supplements Chronicling America with additional Maryland titles.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Legacy of Slavery in Maryland — MSA
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: MSA's signature database for African American genealogy. Indexes enslaved persons, free negro certificates, manumission records, slaveholder records, and related documents across Maryland counties. Maryland was a slave state with a uniquely large free Black population — Baltimore had the largest free Black community of any US city before the Civil War. Essential for any African American research in Maryland.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Georgetown Slavery Archive — Jesuit Plantation Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Georgetown University's documentation of enslaved persons on Jesuit plantations in Maryland, particularly St. Mary's, Charles, and Prince George's counties. The 1838 sale of 272 enslaved persons to Louisiana is extensively documented. Includes plantation ledgers, sacramental records, and family reconstruction efforts.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad Visitor Center / Maryland State Archives / Dorchester County Historical Society
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Records related to Harriet Tubman (born Araminta Ross, c. 1822, Dorchester County) and the Underground Railroad in Maryland. Court records, manumission documents, and community histories for the Eastern Shore's enslaved and free Black populations. NPS Tubman Byway connects historical sites across Dorchester, Caroline, and Talbot counties.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Port of Baltimore Passenger Arrival Records — NARA / Ancestry / FamilySearch
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Baltimore was one of the top US immigration ports, second only to New York/Ellis Island. Passenger arrival lists from the 1820s to 1957 document millions of arrivals including German, Irish, Polish, Italian, Lithuanian, Czech, Greek, and Jewish immigrants. NARA microfilm series M255 (1820-1891) and M596 (1891-1948). Available on Ancestry and FamilySearch. Baltimore was the primary port for German immigrants in the mid-1800s.

Naturalization petitions and declarations of intention from Maryland courts. MSA holds county court naturalization records. NARA holds federal court records. Baltimore City naturalization records are particularly voluminous given the port city's immigrant population. Records date from the late 1700s through 1952.

military_records.description: MSA holds state militia records, adjutant general records, and service records. Maryland Line (Continental Army) records for the Revolution. War of 1812 militia musters and the British burning of Washington (1814). Civil War records — Maryland was a border state with soldiers serving BOTH sides. US Colored Troops regiments recruited in Maryland. WWI and WWII draft registrations at NARA.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System — Maryland
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Maryland soldiers. Maryland contributed soldiers to both Union and Confederate forces as a border state. The Battle of Antietam (1862) and Battle of Monocacy (1864) were fought on Maryland soil.

land_and_property.description: MSA's free online system providing digitized land records for ALL Maryland counties and Baltimore City. Deed books, mortgages, land patents, and related records from the earliest county formations. One of the most comprehensive online land record systems in the United States. Registration required but free. Searchable by grantor/grantee and browsable by book and page.

Colonial-era land patents, warrants, and surveys from the proprietary period (1634-1776). The original land grants from the Lords Baltimore are at MSA. Maryland used the metes and bounds survey system. Patent records include the name of the tract, acreage, and description — tract names are genealogically significant.

vital_records.source_name: Maryland Vital Records — Division of Vital Records / MSA
vital_records.repository: Maryland Department of Health / Maryland State Archives
vital_records.description: State registration of births and deaths began in 1898. Pre-1898 vital events must be researched through church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices. Historical vital records are transferred to MSA over time. Modern vital records from the Maryland Division of Vital Records. Marriage licenses filed with the Clerk of Circuit Court in each county from the colonial era.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Maryland Church Records — Catholic, Episcopal, Quaker, Methodist
church_and_cemetery.repository: Archdiocese of Baltimore / Episcopal Diocese / Swarthmore College / MSA
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are the primary source for vital events before 1898. Maryland's Catholic heritage (founded as a Catholic colony) means Catholic sacramental records are unusually rich — the Archdiocese of Baltimore Archives holds records from the 1790s. Anglican/Episcopal parish records date from 1692 (establishment of parishes). Quaker meeting records from the 1680s are at Swarthmore College. Methodist circuit records from the late 1700s, especially on the Eastern Shore. German Reformed and Lutheran records in Frederick and Washington counties.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Maryland. Coverage varies by county. Includes church cemeteries, family burial grounds, and municipal cemeteries. Many Maryland genealogical societies have published cemetery transcription volumes.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing the Maryland Genealogical Society Bulletin and other research aids. Maintains surname files, Bible record collections, and research guides. Sponsors workshops and lectures on Maryland research.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all Maryland counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Maryland has no extinct counties in the traditional sense. All 23 current counties derive from the original colonial counties established between 1637 and 1872. Some historical changes: Patuxent County was renamed Calvert County in 1658. Baltimore City separated from Baltimore County in 1851 as an independent city. Several counties were carved from larger original counties over time — Frederick County once encompassed all of western Maryland before Washington (1776), Montgomery (1776), Allegany (1789), Carroll (1837), and ultimately Garrett (1872) were separated from it. The colonial hundred system (subdivisions within counties) was used for local administration and tax assessment but hundreds were not separate jurisdictions.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Maryland has only one recognized burned county: Calvert County, whose 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deed books, court minutes, and wills. However, Maryland's record survival is generally excellent due to the Maryland State Archives' centralized preservation efforts. MSA holds state-level copies of many county records that can partially compensate for Calvert's losses. Church records, which survived the fire, are essential for reconstructing Calvert County genealogies.

burned_jurisdictions.record_losses: 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deed books, court minutes, and wills prior to that date
